title: Graphs
description: A tree, generalized fully: multiple connections, real cycles, and the vocabulary — vertex, edge, directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted.
objectives: Understand a tree as a special, restricted case of a graph
Know core graph vocabulary: vertex, edge, directed, undirected, weighted, cycle
Understand what makes a graph fundamentally more general than a tree
Preview graph representation, traversal, and algorithms as upcoming topics

sections.text: 1. Recap: A Tree Was Already a Special Kind of Graph
BFS and DFS were both already introduced as working on "any graph-like structure," with trees as one specific case. Now let's define graphs properly, and see exactly what a tree was restricting.
A graph is a set of vertices (nodes) connected by edges, with no restriction on how many connections a vertex can have, and no requirement that it be free of cycles.

A tree is exactly a graph with two extra restrictions: it's connected (every vertex reachable from every other) and it has no cycles (exactly one path between any two vertices). Remove those restrictions, and you have a general graph.

rows: A single point in the graph
Two vertices directly connected by an edge
A sequence of edges connecting one vertex to another
A path that starts and ends at the same vertex, using no edge twice

sections.code:
// Undirected: A - B means you can travel A -> B AND B -> A
// Directed:   A -> B means you can ONLY travel A -> B, not automatically the reverse

sections.text: A directed graph's edges have a direction — an edge from A to B does not imply a connection from B to A.

sections.title: Two-way streets vs one-way streets
sections.text: An undirected graph is a city of two-way streets — if you can drive from A to B, you can drive back. A directed graph is a city with one-way streets — being able to reach B from A says nothing about whether you can get back the same way.
A weighted graph assigns a numeric cost (a weight) to each edge, representing something like distance, time, or price, rather than treating every connection as equal.

Recall BFS finding shortest path by counting STEPS — that assumes every edge is equally "expensive," an unweighted graph. A weighted graph (like a road network with real distances) needs different algorithms to find the truly cheapest path, since the fewest number of edges isn't necessarily the lowest total cost.

This is exactly why BFS and DFS both needed an explicit visited set once we moved off of trees — a graph can have cycles, meaning a path can loop back to a vertex already seen, something a tree's structure could never do.

sections.title: A social network, not an org chart
sections.text: Recall the org chart from Trees — strictly hierarchical, one path down from any manager to any employee. A social network is a graph instead: any two people might be connected, connections go both ways, friend groups form actual cycles (A knows B, B knows C, C knows A), and there's no single 'root' person everyone traces back to.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming every graph algorithm from Trees (BFS, DFS) works unchanged — the visited set becomes mandatory, not optional, the moment cycles are possible.
Forgetting directed edges aren't automatically two-way — A -> B existing says nothing about B -> A.
Treating a weighted graph's shortest path the same as an unweighted one's — fewest edges (BFS) and lowest total cost (needs different algorithms) can give different answers.
Thinking a tree and a graph are unrelated concepts — a tree is simply a graph with no cycles and full connectivity, a special case, not a separate category.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a graph?
questions.answer: A set of vertices connected by edges, with no restriction on the number of connections per vertex and no requirement of being cycle-free.

questions.question: How does a tree relate to a graph?
questions.answer: A tree is a special case of a graph — one that is connected and has no cycles, giving exactly one path between any two vertices.

questions.question: What is the difference between a directed and an undirected graph?
questions.answer: In an undirected graph, an edge between two vertices implies travel is possible in both directions; in a directed graph, an edge has a direction, and connectivity one way doesn't imply the reverse.

questions.question: Why does BFS's shortest-path guarantee not directly apply to a weighted graph?
questions.answer: BFS counts the fewest number of edges, assuming every edge costs the same; in a weighted graph, the path with the fewest edges isn't necessarily the one with the lowest total cost.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A graph is a set of vertices connected by edges, without a tree's restrictions of being fully connected and cycle-free — a tree is simply a special, more restricted case of a graph. Edges can be directed (one-way, like a city's one-way streets) or undirected (two-way), and weighted (carrying a numeric cost like distance) or unweighted (every edge treated equally). Because general graphs can have cycles, the visited-set requirement introduced for BFS and DFS on grids becomes mandatory rather than optional — and because edges can carry weights, finding the 'shortest' path requires different algorithms depending on whether 'shortest' means fewest edges or lowest total cost.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Graphs
sections.text: Graph = a social network, not an org chart. Tree = a graph with training wheels (connected, no cycles). Directed = one-way streets. Weighted = streets with real distances, not just step counts.
